versions.title: Version History
versions.archive_section_intro: Each version of this dataset is stored as a separate group of files on ODATA. The latest version is always the one with the highest version number; version 1 is the first scrape we ran. Newer versions appear at the top of the list below.
versions.archive_explain_ckan: Each version contains the original files from data.gov.il as they existed when we scraped them, under their original resource names (e.g. "budget.csv"). On ODATA you'll find them under the same name as in the source.
versions.archive_explain_govmap: Each version contains two parallel files: a GeoJSON file with every polygon's geometry, and a CSV file with the same properties without the coordinates (one row per polygon). Both files live in the same version folder on ODATA.
versions.archive_explain_scraper: Each version contains two files: a ZIP archive with every document scraped from the source page, and a CSV index — every row in the CSV points to one document inside the ZIP, with the original file name, source URL, date, and title. To find a specific document, search the CSV and open the matching entry inside the ZIP.
versions.archive_explain_idf: Each version contains two files: a ZIP archive with every document scraped from the IDF Military Prosecution site, and a CSV index — one row per document with the original file name, URL, date, and title.
versions.archive_explain_default: Each version contains the files captured at that point in time. Version 1 is the first scrape; higher version numbers are newer.
versions.delete_confirm: Permanently delete {{label}}? This will remove the associated files from ODATA and cannot be undone.

footer.description: Tracking government open data version history

about.title: About Versions for the People
about.what_text: "Versions for the People" tracks changes to Israeli government and public datasets. It began by watching the open datasets on <1>data.gov.il</1>, and today it also monitors additional sources: the Knesset database, the Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS), GovMap's spatial layers, committee protocols, tenders, government resolutions, and more. When a dataset is updated, the system saves the previous version, letting you observe changes over time and identify when public information was added, removed, or modified.
about.why_text: Government datasets are usually updated by overwriting existing data: once new data is published, the old data is gone. "Versions for the People" preserves the history so transparency and accountability extend beyond the moment of first publication, rather than depending on a single snapshot that may quietly disappear.
about.how_text: The system polls the sources it monitors (including <1>data.gov.il</1>) on a configurable schedule, detects changes, and stores every version so it can be compared and queried via API. Some historical versions are kept on the data infrastructure of "Information for the People" (<2>odata.org.il</2>).
about.for_whom_item1: Journalists and researchers who need to prove that government data has changed.
about.for_whom_item2: Civil society organisations watching datasets that might quietly erode.
about.for_whom_item3: Developers and analysts who need a dataset's full history, not just the current snapshot.
about.what_can_do_item1: Track specific datasets and see every change they’ve gone through.
about.what_can_do_item2: Compare versions of the same dataset.
about.what_can_do_item3: Surface silent changes that were never officially announced.
about.who_text: The project is led by Adv. Gai Zomer, founder of the Octopus Association, the Hatzlacha Association, and the Movement for Freedom of Information. It is part of a family of activist initiatives at the intersection of data, law and technology. See all projects at <1>z-g.co.il/projects.
about.family_intro: "Versions for the People" is part of a family of projects that aim to make public information accessible and improve government transparency in Israel:
about.family_odata_desc: The civil society's open data portal, and one of the data infrastructures on which "Versions for the People" stores historical versions.
about.family_ocoi_name: Conflicts of Interest for the People
about.family_ocoi_desc: Centralises and publishes conflict-of-interest arrangements of public officials, with a visual relationship map.
about.family_ocal_desc: A unified calendar of meeting diaries of elected officials and senior civil servants.
about.contact_text: For data leaks, accessibility issues, comments and feedback: <1>guy@z-g.co.il</1>.
about.rationale_teaser: Why does this project exist at all? On Government Resolution 1933, the "Open by Default" policy and the gap between promise and implementation, see the <1>rationale page</1>.

rationale.title: Open by Default
rationale.subtitle: What the State of Israel committed to in Government Resolution 1933 — and what happened in the decade since
rationale.promise_text1: On August 30, 2016, the Israeli government adopted <1>Resolution No. 1933</1> — "Improving Government Information Transfer and Making Government Databases Accessible to the Public". Its first part (sections 1–11) dealt with data sharing between ministries (the "Once Only" policy); section 12 onwards deals with us — the public. In section 12 the government adopted the recommendations of the <2>inter-ministerial team report on opening government databases</2> (July 2016), setting a policy built around a single principle: government databases are a public resource, so opening them is the rule and withholding them is the exception — Open by Default.
rationale.promise_text2: Among the obligations the government took upon itself:
rationale.promise_item1: Open all government databases by 2022 — including any new database created after that date (section 12(a)).
rationale.promise_item2: Map every database in every ministry by the end of 2017, with a multi-year plan for their gradual publication (section 12(b)).
rationale.promise_item3: Publish, already by the end of 2017, databases designated as especially valuable: real-time transport data, the Ministry of Education budget per school, the Survey of Israel parcel index, Israel Police crime data, the planning inventory and municipal budgets (section 12(c)).
rationale.promise_item4: Appoint within 30 days, in every ministry, a senior official responsible for implementation, and report annually which databases were opened — and which were not, with reasons (sections 12(d)–(e)).
rationale.promise_item5: Allocate a dedicated budget, including NIS 15 million per year to incentivize ministries that open databases (section 13).
rationale.promise_item6: Stop charging the public for data from government databases, except statutory fees and approved exceptions (section 14).
rationale.standard_title: The Technological Standard: Not Just Publishing — True Accessibility
rationale.standard_text1: The resolution did not stop at declarations. Appendix C — "Principles for Opening Databases" — anchored the technological standard drafted by the inter-ministerial team, defining exactly how a database must be opened:
rationale.standard_item1: In an open, freely processable format (such as CSV, XML, JSON) — enabling retrieval, processing, distribution and reuse.
rationale.standard_item2: Following accepted international standards, including access via an Application Programming Interface (API).
rationale.standard_item3: Accompanied by metadata and proper documentation — both a description of the dataset for discovery, and a description of each field and its meaning.
rationale.standard_item4: Up-to-date, complete and detailed data, with ongoing, systematic checks after publication to ensure it stays current and accessible.
rationale.standard_item5: Under an open license, free of charge, in one central place — data.gov.il.
rationale.standard_text2: In other words: the state itself determined that every public database must be machine-readable, documented, current and free. This is not a civil-society wish list — it is a government resolution.
rationale.exceptions_title: The Exceptions — Narrow, Reasoned, and With a Reversed Burden
rationale.exceptions_text1: The resolution recognized that not every database can be opened, but drew the exceptions narrowly: identifiable personal information (as defined in the Privacy Protection Law), a legal impediment, or other relevant considerations such as information security. Appendix C even required that where a database of public importance contains identifiable data, the ministry must anonymize it so it can still be opened.
rationale.exceptions_text2: And even when an exception applies — the burden is on the ministry: section 12(f) requires a ministry that cannot publish a database in an open format to consider alternative access (query systems, research rooms), and section 12(d) requires an annual report on every database left closed — with the reasons. The logic was reversed: the citizen no longer has to justify why they deserve the data — the ministry has to justify why not.
rationale.reality_text1: The deadline set by the resolution — 2022 — has passed. The State Comptroller's audit of its implementation (<1>"Making Government and Public Databases Accessible to the Public", May 2021</1>) found, among other things:
rationale.reality_item1: About two-thirds of government ministries failed to comply with the resolution.
rationale.reality_item2: About a third of ministries never completed mapping their own databases — so no one even knows what exists.
rationale.reality_item3: More than 10% of ministries opened not even a single database.
rationale.reality_item4: Actual budgets allocated were 30%–80% lower than the amount the resolution mandated.
rationale.reality_item5: Even from the short list of databases explicitly ordered opened by the end of 2017 — Israel Police crime data was never published.
rationale.reality_text2: And beyond the numbers — the reality familiar to anyone working with Israeli government data: a huge share of public databases, with no legal barrier to their publication, is simply not accessible. Data is trapped in closed query systems that block automated access; files are overwritten on every update with no history kept; datasets vanish from data.gov.il without notice; and field documentation — the standard set in Appendix C — is the exception, not the rule.
rationale.principle: Opening government databases is the rule. Withholding them is the exception.
rationale.principle_source: The "Open by Default" principle adopted in section 12 of Government Resolution 1933
rationale.fix_title: When the State Doesn't Implement — the Public Does
rationale.fix_text1: "Versions for the People" and the "Government Jester" browser extension were not created to replace the state, but to actually carry out what it decided — and failed to do:
rationale.fix_over_desc: Fulfills the preservation, currency and documentation duties: it tracks government datasets, saves every historical version (normally overwritten at the source), documents the changes and republishes everything — including an open API and documentation — to the very standard Appendix C demanded of the ministries themselves.
rationale.fix_jester_desc: Fulfills the open-format duty: it turns databases trapped in query systems and viewing interfaces — gov.il, Nadlan, GovMap, the Planning Administration and more — into open CSV and GeoJSON files, in one click, directly in the user's browser.
rationale.fix_text2: Every database the extension frees from a closed system, and every historical version preserved here, implements one more clause of Resolution 1933. That is the whole rationale.
rationale.source_decision: Government Resolution No. 1933, "Improving Government Information Transfer and Making Government Databases Accessible to the Public" (Aug 30, 2016, Hebrew)
rationale.source_report: Final Report — the Inter-Ministerial Team on Opening Government Databases to the Public (July 2016, PDF, Hebrew)
rationale.source_comptroller: State Comptroller, "Making Government and Public Databases Accessible to the Public" (May 2021, Hebrew)
rationale.source_calcalist: Calcalist: "About two-thirds of government ministries failed to open their databases" (2021, Hebrew)
rationale.source_jester: The Government Jester — a Chrome extension for downloading Israeli government datasets
rationale.source_about: The About page — what this system is and how it works

map.title: Layer map
map.no_filterable_fields: No filterable fields in this layer
map.mobile_gate_body: This layer may contain hundreds of thousands of polygons. Loading all of them on a mobile device may be slow and can crash the browser. You can filter first and load only a subset.
map.filter_first_hint: Pick which values to display on the map, then tap "Load filtered". You can pick as many as you like — if you pick none, every polygon is loaded.
map.estimating_filter_match: Showing {{visible}} of {{total}} polygons matching the current filter

growth.title: Agricultural Crops
growth.subtitle: Curated view of Israeli agricultural parcels by crop type
growth.diagnostics_summary: {{matched}} of {{total}} source polygons fell into a configured layer
growth.diag_top_unmatched_hint: To include them too, add the matching string to growthnameMatches in frontend/src/config/growthLayers.ts

api.title: Public API
api.subtitle: Open access to the data — datasets, versions, organizations and tags
api.intro: All endpoints are public GETs. No auth, no API key required.
api.intro_2: Open for researchers, journalists, transparency activists, and civic projects. If you are building a heavy integration, please avoid millions of parallel calls and keep a local cache.

cbs.title: Central Bureau of Statistics (CBS)
cbs.subtitle: A searchable index of the CBS website (cbs.gov.il): publications, releases, tables and downloadable files. Search runs over page content; the files themselves live on the CBS site and open directly from there.
cbs.placeholder: Search CBS content — e.g. "Consumer Price Index"
cbs.subject: Subject
cbs.no_results: No results found in the CBS index.
cbs.featured_admin_hint: No pinned pages yet. Search for a page and click the star (☆) to pin it here.
